items.q: What is the most common informal way to form a yes/no question in French?
items.correct: Rising intonation (statement said as a question) or using 'est-ce que'.
items.why: Students learn formal inversion first. In conversation, French uses intonation or est-ce que far more often. Inversion sounds formal/written - 'Tu parles français?' is the everyday form.

items.q: How do you say 'I'm going to my friend's house' in French?
items.misconception: Je vais à la maison de mon ami.
items.why: Students translate word-for-word using 'à la maison de'. 'Chez' specifically means 'at the home/place of' someone: chez moi, chez le médecin, chez Pierre. Much more idiomatic and tested.

items.q: What does 'mes parents' mean in French?
items.correct: My parents (mother and father specifically) - but 'parents' can also mean relatives more broadly.
items.misconception: It always means all my relatives.
items.why: Students think French 'parents' = English 'relatives' because in some contexts it does. Usually 'mes parents' = mother+father; 'mes proches' or 'ma famille' = relatives more broadly. Context matters.
It's Sister. “Brother” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How do you say 'I have' in French?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: J'ai. “Je suis” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many family terms are listed in this lesson?
items.why: Exact answer: 6. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
